New report finds tax cuts benefit highest-income Idaho households most
entry.summary: CALDWELL — A Caldwell man faces felony charges after he alle… A Canyon County man may spend the rest of his life in prison… The Capitol dome stands against the Boise foothills, Tuesday, April 7, 2026. Originally published June 18 on IdahoCapitalSun.com. A new report published this month by a Boise-based nonprofit organization found that state and federal income tax cuts have benefited the highest-income Idaho households the most, while low-income families are actually paying more. The Idaho Center for Fiscal Policy published the findings in the new report, “How federal and state tax cuts shift costs onto Idaho families and communities.” The report’s author, Policy Analyst May Roberts, tracked $4.5 billion in annual state and federal income tax cuts that Idaho households have received since 2018. The report found that 73% of the benefits of those cuts went to the top 20% of households, those that earn $143,000 or more each year. The report also found that Idahoans with the lowest incomes – those earning less than $29,200 annually – saw their taxes increase by an average of $96 per year. Since 2021, the Idaho Legislature has enacted several tax cuts that have reduced state revenue. Here are a few examples: The increase in taxes among the lowest-income earners was due, in part, to the Idaho Legislature allowing the state child tax credit to expire and the federal One Big Beautiful Bill Act not extending premium tax credits that helped Idaho families and small business owners afford health insurance, the report stated. “Tax cuts can be a valuable tool for helping families make ends meet, but it is important to look at who benefits and who bears the costs,” Roberts said in a written statement. “A fair tax system can provide meaningful tax relief while maintaining the essential services Idaho families rely on. When tax cuts significantly reduce the resources available for public services, the costs do not disappear — they are shifted onto families and local communities through higher property taxes, rising tuition and fees, reduced services and growing barriers to accessing healthcare and food assistance.” Sen. Scott Grow, a Republican from Eagle who serves as co-chair of the Idaho Legislature’s Joint Finance-Appropriations Committee, supports the tax cuts. “I am a fiscal conservative,” Grow said. “As (certified public accountants), we try to help our clients to make sure they pay a fair amount of tax and not overly pay. What we are hearing from our constituents is that they are concerned about high taxes, whether it be property tax or income tax. We try to be very fiscally conservative. I believe in limited government. We need to fund the government appropriately, and I feel like we do that.” The report also found that the combined $4.5 billion in tax cuts exceeds the Idaho Legislature’s fiscal year 2026 state funding for K-12 public education, colleges and universities and health and human services. The report said that for fiscal year 2026, there was $2.2 billion in state funding for K-12 public education, $1.2 billion for health and human services and $492 million for colleges and universities. After the Idaho Legislature cut funding for colleges in universities in fiscal year 2026 and fiscal year 2027, the Idaho State Board of Education approved tuition increases, Idaho Education News reported. “These examples illustrate how state-level budget cuts can shift costs to local governments and directly onto Idahoans,” the Idaho Center for Fiscal Policy’s new report states. More information about the Idaho Center for Fiscal Policy and its research is available online. Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! To view our latest e-Edition, click the image on the left.

entry.title: Canyon County man to serve decades in prison for child sex abuse material crime
entry.summary: CALDWELL — A Caldwell man faces felony charges after he alle… BOISE — The U.S. Marshals Service’s Greater Idaho Fugitive T… A Canyon County man may spend the rest of his life in prison for distributing child sex abuse material. Daniel Whiteley, age 47, was sentenced on June 8 to serve a sentence of 40 years to life after pleading guilty to knowingly distributing child sex abuse material, or CSAM, the Idaho Attorney General’s Office announced. According to online court records, Whiteley — a registered sex offender — was initially charged with one count of distributing CSAM, five counts of possessing or accessing CSAM, one count of destruction of evidence and a persistent violator enhancement. The charges were filed between August 2024 and September 2025. Whiteley pleaded not guilty to the charges before ultimately agreeing to a plea deal, pleading guilty to the distributing CSAM charge, online court records show. The attorney general’s office noted that the crime to which Whiteley pleaded guilty is punishable by up to 30 years in prison; however, Whiteley’s persistent violator enhancement allowed for a longer sentence. Whiteley pleaded guilty to rape in 2000. The AG’s office stated that the investigation into Whiteley started after the Internet Crimes Against Children (ICAC) Unit received a CyberTip from the National Center for Missing and Exploited Children. The tip was generated by a popular social media platform and indicated that a user — subsequently identified as Whiteley — shared child sexual abuse material with another user via direct messaging. Whiteley’s bio on the social media platform read: “46-year-old single m[ale] from Idaho, just another dirty old man perv.” Law enforcement obtained and executed a search warrant for Whiteley’s residence and, the AG’s office states, upon entering the residence, law enforcement saw Whiteley come out of a room where officers found a recently broken laptop. A digital forensic analysis of the laptop located over 1,000 files of child sexual abuse material. The children in the files ranged from approximately 2 years old to teenagers and many were being sexually abused by adults. “This is exactly the kind of predator who has no business walking free in our communities,” Attorney General Raúl Labrador stated. “Every file on that laptop was a real child being abused. My office will keep working with local law enforcement to find these criminals, build strong cases, and put them away for as long as the law allows.” In her sentencing judgment, District Judge Cheri C. Copsey reasoned that “Whiteley is not amenable to treatment, citing particular concern with the number of files found on Whiteley’s computer and his lack of accountability,” the AG’s office said. Madison Guernsey is the News Editor at the Idaho Press. Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! entry.title: Garden City gets $2M for drinking water
entry.summary: A Canyon County man may spend the rest of his life in prison… CALDWELL — A vegetation fire in northwest Caldwell prompted … The “Welcome to Garden City” sign is pictured in this 2022 file photo. GARDEN CITY — The Idaho Department of Environmental Quality (DEQ) has awarded more than $2 million to Garden City to help the city invest in drinking water infrastructure. The $2,179,100 in funding will “support the design and drilling of a new drinking water well free of emerging contaminants,” according to a DEQ release. The release notes that emerging contaminants “are substances that may pose newly identified or re-emerging risks to human health or the environment. Examples include per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (PFAS), pharmaceuticals and personal care products, and microplastics.” The loan carries a simple 0% interest rate and $2,179,100 in principal forgiveness, the release states. The monies are from DEQ’s State Revolving Loan Fund and capitalized annually by grants from the United States Environmental Protection Agency’s Emerging Contaminants program, which is funded through the Bipartisan Infrastructure Law. Madison Guernsey is the News Editor at the Idaho Press. Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! entry.title: Crews work to contain fire in Caldwell
entry.summary: GARDEN CITY — The Idaho Department of Environmental Quality … MERIDIAN — George Seybold, CEO of Meridian’s Talloo marketin… A vegetation fire Wednesday in Caldwell was responded to by 80 firefighters from multiple agencies. CALDWELL — A vegetation fire in northwest Caldwell prompted the actions of dozens of fire fighters across multiple agencies Wednesday. The blaze burning around Look Lane and Highway 26 at the Idaho Materials and Construction site affected approximately 30 acres, according to a news release from the Caldwell Fire Department, and while initial reports indicated that several structures were threatened, crews stopped the fire’s progress before it caused any structure loss. No injuries to firefighters or civilians have been reported. At the incident’s peak, approximately 80 personnel were assigned to the fire. The fire was nearing containment as of Wednesday night, CFD said, adding that crews were expected to remain on scene throughout the night “working in medium to heavy fuels adjacent to the Boise River.” “Crews will continue extinguishing hotspots, strengthening containment lines, and monitoring for any potential flare-ups,” Caldwell Fire stated. “Personnel will likely patrol the fire area over the coming days until significant moisture or precipitation reduces the risk of rekindles.” Caldwell Fire was aided in its response by fire crews from Nampa, Middleton-Star, Wilder, Parma, Fruitland, Sand Hollow, Boise, New Plymouth, Payette, the Whitney Fire District and Caldwell Rural Fire District. Additional assistance was provided by the Bureau of Land Management, Canyon County Emergency Management and Canyon County Paramedics. The cause of the fire remains under investigation. “The Caldwell Fire Department extends its appreciation to all assisting agencies and personnel whose coordinated efforts helped protect lives and property during this incident,” the release states. Madison Guernsey is the News Editor at the Idaho Press. Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! entry.title: Letting them cook: Owyhee students try their hand at business with recipe website
entry.summary: CALDWELL — A vegetation fire in northwest Caldwell prompted … BOISE — Back on track for railroad enthusiasts, the U.S. Dep… Owyhee High School sophmores Mason Seybold and Thomas Day work on their laptops at Talloo Business Hub in Meridian, Tuesday, June 23, 2026. Owyhee High School sophmores Mason Seybold and Thomas Day pause for a photo while working on their website at Talloo Business Hub in Meridian, Tuesday, June 23, 2026. Owyhee High School sophmores Mason Seybold and Thomas Day work on their laptops at Talloo Business Hub in Meridian, Tuesday, June 23, 2026. MERIDIAN — George Seybold, CEO of Meridian’s Talloo marketing firm, issued a summer challenge to his 15-year-old son Mason and his friend Thomas Day. “You’re going to create a business. You take a maximum of one month. I’m going to guide you and help you figure this thing out,” the elder Seybold said. “These young men are saying, ‘how do we do something that’s better than playing video games over the summer?’” George Seybold, CEO of Meridian’s Talloo marketing firm, checks in on his 15-year-old son Mason and his friend Thomas Day as the duo work on their website, Tuesday, June 23, 2026. The teens, incoming sophomores at Owyhee High School, settled on a recipe website as both enjoy cooking. Mason likes to make chocolate chip cookies, while Thomas says he’s “a pasta guy.” They were motivated to created a repository of recipes that were easily accessible to would-be chefs, without ads or lengthy memoirs from bloggers. “We don’t have any of the crazy pop-ups and crazy stories, so it creates a user-friendly experience for people who want to cook,” Thomas said. “A lot of the stories I see, it’s always some sort of family member that passed it down for generations, but they always make it so extremely detailed that I have to scroll for two minutes just to get to the recipe.” Called Le Sapor — Latin for “flavor” — the website currently contains about 700 recipes, from Korean bean pancakes to chicken noodle soup, with more to come. A screenshot from the recipe website lesapor.com. Instead of conventional advertising, the site utilizes a pay-per-click model in which local food producers such as farmers and produce vendors can pay a monthly subscription fee and sign up to embed certain ingredient words in recipes with purchase links to their websites. “(Producers) can sign up for a business account, and we have this one page that lists the producers that exist and their business,” Mason said. “They can get recognition that way, while people are looking through it, or when somebody clicks on one of the recipe ingredients.” The site is on the verge of linking up its first producers, George Seybold said, about a week after it first went active. “We just wanted to acknowledge a bunch of people that put in effort for someone in a home with food,” Thomas said. Owyhee High School sophmores Mason Seybold and Thomas Day work on their laptops at Talloo Business Hub in Meridian, Tuesday, June 23, 2026. One feature of Le Sapor is “recipe mode,” which users can activate when they don’t want to physically touch their phone or computer due to messy fingers. In recipe mode, screens won’t fall asleep and a chef can swipe up or down with motions in front of the camera. Cooks can access the Le Sapor catalog and submit recipes of their own at lesapor.com.

entry.title: Boise grant seeks to restore passenger trains
entry.summary: Something’s in the Treasure Valley air as the weather is exp… Initial motions were made by Boise City Council regarding th… Visitors walk along a path toward the Boise Depot to celebrate the century-old local icon on Wednesday, April 16, 2025. BOISE — Back on track for railroad enthusiasts, the U.S. Department of Transportation has awarded the city of Boise half-a-million dollars to pursue intercity passenger rail service in the city’s future. The grant will allow the city to develop a financing strategy for future passenger rail opportunities, a Wednesday city press release states. For Boise, it will allow the city to look at the Boise Depot and “other station-area investments.” The $503,125 grant comes from the department’s Innovative Financing and Asset Concessions Program which helps communities explore creative approaches to transportation, according to the city. Attempts have been made in recent years by local leaders to revive a line from the Boise Depot to Portland and Salt Lake. However, a mistake in 2023 cost the Idaho Transportation Department a $500,000 grant that would have funded the study, as reported by the Idaho Capital Sun. This new grant will provide a similar opportunity. “Boiseans have made clear they want to bring passenger rail back to get around the Treasure Valley and across the West,” Mayor Lauren McLean said in the release. “This grant helps bring us one step closer to doing just that.” Haadiya Tariq is the Boise reporter for the Idaho Press. You can follow her on socials @HaadiyaTariq or email her at . Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! entry.title: 12-year-old boy dies after incident at Idaho hot springs
entry.summary: BOISE — Back on track for railroad enthusiasts, the U.S. Dep… BOISE — The impending expansion of the Boise Centre has cont… FEATHERVILLE — A Treasure Valley family is grieving following the death of a boy over the weekend at an Idaho hot springs. Everett Newby, a 12-year-old Boise boy, died Saturday, June 20, at St. Luke’s in Boise after he was “submerged in water for a period of time” at Trinity Hot Springs in Featherville, the Ada County Coroner’s Office announced Wednesday. Newby’s cause and manner of death are listed as pending. The hot springs are located 100 miles east of Boise in Elmore County. An online fundraiser for the boy’s family states that Newby was taken to the hospital via life flight, and he died in the ICU, the coroner’s office stated. As of Wednesday evening, the fundraiser had raised nearly $4,000. Madison Guernsey is the News Editor at the Idaho Press. entry.title: Boise Centre growth continues with third land acquisition
entry.summary: FEATHERVILLE — A Treasure Valley family is grieving followin… MERIDIAN — Former Idaho state Rep. Dorothy Moon was elected … The entrance to the Boise Centre is pictured in this file photo. A real estate hospitality firm has recommended the Greater Boise Auditorium District either expand the center or erect a new multi-purpose indoor sports facility, among other options, as event space in Boise continues to grow. A commercial building stands at the northeast corner of 11th and River streets in Boise, Tuesday, April 7, 2026. The Greater Boise Auditorium District purchased the 4-acre property — plus an additional 1.8-acre parcel — to serve as a stepping stone for the expansion of the Boise Centre. BOISE — The impending expansion of the Boise Centre has continued to grow in size with the purchase of yet another downtown plot. The purchase was approved on Tuesday by the Greater Boise Auditorium District Board, according to a press release. Since a 2025 study by Johnson Consulting recommended the Boise Centre double its event space, the district has acquired land in the downtown area with a mix of possible venues in mind. “As we look to the future of the district and the needs it can serve in the next 10 years and beyond, this collection of properties expands the opportunities to build something that serves the Boise community across multiple facets and invests in the future of downtown Boise,” Board Chair Kristin Muchow said in the release. The center is landlocked among skyscrapers and other developments in the middle of downtown Boise, leaving few options to expand the existing facility. The work-around is to build somewhere else. The three parcels that have been purchased by the district this year are a few blocks away from the Boise Centre, approximately an eight-minute walk. The construction of a new facility will not impact the current operations of the center. The three parcels acquired for future expansion by the Greater Boise Auditorium District are southwest of the Boise Centre. “A key attribute we are looking for with this future project is avoiding becoming landlocked as we are with our current facility,” district Executive Director Cody Lund said in the release. “Through the purchase agreements with all three parcels, we now have the flexibility to imagine a second property that not only expands our current book of convention business but opens up the possibilities for further community collaboration and engagement.” The first parcel was purchased in early April and is a 4-acre lot near the western corner of the 10th and River streets intersection. The second parcel, a 1.8-acre lot, was acquired at the end of April and is just across the street, east of 10th Street. This third lot is on the corner of 9th and Lee streets and adds approximately half an acre of land. The combined three parcels are a total of nearly 6.5 acres. According to the press release, the third parcel purchase “completes the district’s exploratory committee’s initial discussions and evaluations to secure land.” According to Lund, the district does not currently have plans to purchase any additional land. “The district’s exploratory committee has met with numerous landowners, developers, and civic leaders about land opportunities in and around Boise Centre in the past 12 months,” Lund wrote in an email. “At this time, there are no active plans to move forward on additional land, though the district remains open to opportunities that benefit the future development project as we work through what the site components and design could include.” The Boise Centre was last expanded in 2017 to accommodate larger events and more events simultaneously, according to the district’s website. A decade later, it currently functions at near full capacity. The 2025 study by Johnson Consulting found that the 86,000-square-foot center loses an average of 115 events annually due to a lack of available space. Options previously presented to the Greater Boise Auditorium District Board included the construction of new exhibit halls or a two-phase project to also construct ballrooms and meeting space in tandem. Nothing has been finalized. According to the release, the auditorium district will be seeking proposals for studies on the site and affordability of a development. This includes considerations for lobby spaces, kitchen, back of house storage, loading docks and a parking structure. The studies will evaluate the best layouts to maximize efficiency and financial viability. Results are expected to be finalized by the end of the year. “This is the first step in the site evaluation process to determine how different components of a multi-use event facility could best fit on the 6.4 acres,” the release states. “... Future project development for the district is an investment in growth alongside current facilities.”

entry.title: Dorothy Moon reelected to third term as Idaho GOP chair
entry.summary: BOISE — The impending expansion of the Boise Centre has cont… EAGLE — City council members in Eagle have voted to outlaw f… MERIDIAN — Former Idaho state Rep. Dorothy Moon was elected to her third term as chair of the Idaho Republican Party during last week’s Idaho GOP Convention in Meridian. Moon announced her candidacy for a third term as chair earlier this month after first being elected in 2022 and again in 2024. In this year’s election, Moon received 306 votes, former Idaho state Sen. Steven Thayn received 155 votes and Mark Fuller, the previous Idaho GOP first vice chair, came in third with 134 votes. In receiving just over 51% of the vote, Moon was able to narrowly avoid a runoff. In his challenge to Moon, Thayn had called for fostering a greater sense of unity among members of the party. “Unity does not mean uniformity of opinion,” Thayn said in a prior news release on his candidacy. “Nor does it mean forced agreement. Forced unity always leads to tyranny. Real unity is voluntary. It is built through listening, respect, and a sincere effort to solve problems together.” In the initial announcement of Moon’s campaign, she highlighted improvements to voter turnout and the defeat of the 2024 ranked-choice voting ballot initiative as among key party successes during her most recent term. “It has been an honor to serve Idaho Republicans during a time of tremendous growth and energy within our party,” Moon said in the release. “Together, we have worked to keep Idaho the conservative leader of the nation, defend election integrity, support our county parties and grassroots volunteers, and elect Republicans who stand firmly for the values of faith, family, freedom, and limited government.” The Idaho GOP did not respond to multiple requests seeking comment from Moon on her victory and the party’s future direction. Platform changes adopted last week include direction for the Idaho State Legislature to eliminate property taxes in the state. In the near term, the attention of the party is once again turning to pending ballot initiatives on medical cannabis and abortion that, like ranked-choice voting, are being opposed by the Idaho Republican Party. “Idaho Republicans share a common commitment to protecting our state from the failed policies we see spreading across the country,” Moon said in the release. “Now is the time to continue building a strong, unified organization capable of winning elections, protecting our most vulnerable, and preserving Idaho’s unique way of life for future generations.” Royce McCandless is the Statehouse reporter and covers Idaho politics. You can email him at . Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! To view our latest e-Edition, click the image on the left. entry.title: Citing severe fire threat, Eagle council members ban fireworks in parts of foothills
entry.summary: MERIDIAN — Former Idaho state Rep. Dorothy Moon was elected … BOISE — The U.S. Marshals Service’s Greater Idaho Fugitive T… This map shows the areas designated as the Wildland-Urban Interface Overlay District, where fireworks have been banned. EAGLE — City council members in Eagle have voted to outlaw fireworks in areas of the foothills that present a severe fire threat. The council approved the resolution Tuesday declaring a restriction on the use of fireworks in areas designated as the Wildland-Urban Interface Overlay District (WUI), which encompasses the Eagle foothills north of Beacon Light Road between Highway 16 and Highway 55, as well as the AdaEagle Bike Park, according to a city of Eagle news release. Those areas contain vegetative conditions that constitute a severe wildfire threat during fire season — approximately June 1 through Oct. 31. The release notes that with the recent mild winter and historically low snowpack, the vegetation in the foothills has dried earlier and become a significantly greater risk to wildfire. “We are not trying to take away the fun of blowing-off fireworks, we’re talking about the worst of the worst areas — and it is a severe fire threat,” Council Member Robert Gillis said at the Tuesday meeting. Ada County commissioners last week voted to ban fireworks in unincorporated areas of the county, some of which overlap with the Eagle WUI. “If you see smoke, make a phone call. Don’t assume it’s a controlled burn — if you see something, say something,” Mayor Brad Pike said during the meeting. Madison Guernsey is the News Editor at the Idaho Press. ADA COUNTY — Wildfire threats stemming from drought, low snowpack and early summer heat waves persuaded the Ada County Board of Commissioners … Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. entry.title: New Idaho laws on housing, social media and executions take effect July 1
entry.summary: NAMPA — Seventeen years after the first semester with academ… The 60th God and Country Festival will bring military recogn… Legislators at work on the floor of the Idaho Senate as the end of the legislative session draws near Tuesday, March 31, 2026. The legislation passed during the 2026 session is due to take effect on July 1. Legislation, passed during the 2026 legislative session, is due to take effect on July 1 as we enter the new fiscal year. More than 700 bills were introduced over the course of what was a particularly busy 2026 legislative session that broached issues of social media, zoning codes, property rights and other issues. Below is just some of the legislation that passed through the Idaho Legislature and is due to take effect at the start of the fiscal year on July 1. Short-term rentalsHouse Bill 583 was the third, and ultimately successful, effort from Rep. Jordan Rodman, R-Coeur d’Alene, to change how local government can regulate short-term rental properties in their communities. The legislation will primarily impact short-term rental property owners and marketplaces, such as Airbnb and Vrbo, by limiting when they can face specific regulations or taxes. Homes offering short-term rental availability stand along Franklin Street near downtown Boise in February. House Bill 583 will change how local government can regulate short-term rental properties in their communities, primarily impacting short-term rental property owners and marketplaces, such as Airbnb and Vrbo, by limiting when they can face Counties and cities will now only be able to enact ordinances on short-term rentals to address a variety of safety requirements, including those for smoke alarms, occupancy limits and carbon monoxide detectors. Cities and counties will also no longer be able to require specific licenses, fees or permits for the operation of short-term rentals and can only subject these properties to existing ordinances — such as those for noise, parking or curfew — when they are applicable to non-short-term-rental uses as well. Zoning code changesIn an effort to respond to the state’s high demand for housing, Senate Bill 1352 prohibits local zoning codes from barring compact, single-family housing developments. The change will apply to cities with populations exceeding 10,000 and requires starter home subdivisions, defined as subdivisions of four or more acres with lots less than or equal to 1,500 square feet, to be allowed within residential zones. Senate Bill 1354 was enacted with a similar aim. Cities of 10,000 or more will have to remove bans on accessory dwelling units in residential zones. Affected cities will need to allow at least one ADU per lot, allow them to be as large as 75% of the primary dwelling and not require additional parking in most circumstances. After previous efforts died in committee, Senate Bill 1356 laid out new rules for how the elections undertaken by the Ada County Highway District (ACHD) — the only countywide highway district in the state — will be carried out in the future. Starting in the 2028 election cycle, ACHD will keep its five sub-districts, but commissioners will run in partisan and districtwide elections after previously only being elected by the residents of their district and acting as a non-partisan body since its inception. Teachers union funding restrictionsHouse Bill 516 implements significant restrictions on how teachers unions for Idaho public school districts will be able to use taxpayer funds. Moving forward, public schools cannot provide taxpayer funds for teachers unions, including the use of paid time off to partake in “teachers union activities.” These are defined broadly and include supporting or opposing candidates seeking public office, influencing the passage or defeat of legislation, regulation, ordinances or ballot measures and preparing, conducting or attending teachers union events. The legislation will apply to all contracts between teachers unions and school districts negotiated, amended or extended after July 1. Firing squad execution changeHouse Bill 37 made firing squad the primary method of execution in the state, and made Idaho the first state in the nation to do so. The change to the state execution law arrived after instances of lethal injection being unavailable and botched execution resulted in executions being called off or the expiration of death warrants. The Idaho Department of Correction currently has eight inmates held under a death sentence.

entry.title: U.S. Supreme Court upholds Idaho law barring transgender females from participating in school female sports teams
entry.summary: New Idaho laws taking effect July 1 will change rules involv… BOISE — The Ada County Coroner’s Office has released the ide… The U.S. Supreme Court is seen Monday, June 29, 2026, in Washington. BOISE — The U.S. Supreme Court unanimously ruled in favor of Idaho on Tuesday, upholding a state law that bans transgender women and girls from participating on schools’ female sports teams. The ruling stems from a legal challenge to Idaho’s Fairness in Women’s Sports Act, which was sponsored by Idaho Falls Rep. Barbara Ehardt and passed in 2020, and prevents transgender women from participating in sports that align with their gender identity at Idaho’s public schools. The challenge was initially filed by then-Boise State University student Lindsay Hecox, who identifies as female and began taking hormones in college. Prior to filing the suit, Hecox had played for the university’s club soccer team and tried out for the women’s Division I track and cross-country teams. Lindsay Hecox poses for a photo at a park in Boise on Tuesday, April 14, 2020. Hecox contended that Idaho’s law stood in violation of the Equal Protection Clause of the 14th Amendment, a position represented by the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU), the ACLU of Idaho, Legal Voice and Cooley LLP over the course of a legal battle that has spanned more than five years. A representative for Boise State University declined to comment on the matter, instead redirecting inquiries to the Idaho Attorney General’s office. The university was not the subject of the lawsuit filed by Hecox. The case taken up by the Supreme Court ruled in favor of laws passed in Idaho and West Virginia, supporting the position that restrictions on transgender sports participation doesn’t violate Title IX law, which prohibits discrimination based on sex in education as well as school athletics. Rendering a final decision on the matter, the court’s majority opinion delivered by Justice Brett Kavanaugh found that equal opportunity in athletics has been maintained through having separate teams for male and female athletes. Equally opening up teams to male and female participants “would deny equal opportunity to female athletes because ... females and males have inherent physical differences relevant to athletic performance.” ”The question before the Court is under Title IX and the Equal Protection Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment, may schools maintain women’s and girls’ sports for biological females? In other words, may schools determine eligibility for women’s and girls’ sports based on biological sex?” Kavanaugh wrote. “The answer is yes.” Previous rulings on the issue of transgender women and girls participating in Idaho schools’ sports saw the U.S. District Court for the District of Idaho grant a preliminary injunction to bar the enforcement of Idaho’s law, the action that was affirmed by both the U.S. Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit before arriving at the U.S. Supreme Court. In October last year, Hecox requested the U.S. District Court to allow her to drop her case as she no longer had plans to play in women’s sports at Boise State. District Judge David Nye ruled to maintain the case, contending there was public interest in seeing a ruling made on the matter, as was previously reported by Idaho Education News. Though Tuesday’s ruling now permits restrictions of sports participation by transgender females to be up to the states, major athletic bodies have already been implementing policy changes to address the matter. In 2025 the NCAA prohibited biologically male students, including those who identified as female and took hormone treatment, from competing on women’s teams. Similar prohibitions have since been made by the U.S. Olympic and Paralympic Committee as well as the International Olympic Committee, as was highlighted in the court’s opinion. The ruling from the nation’s highest court, which aligns with these broad policy changes, was roundly applauded by much of Idaho’s elected leadership on Tuesday. ”Idaho led the nation by becoming the first state to protect women’s sports, and I’ve never wavered in defending that law,” Idaho Attorney General Raúl Labrador said in a written statement. “The Supreme Court has now confirmed that states can preserve fair competition and protect the opportunities that generations of women fought to secure.” “Idaho was the very first state to ban men from participating in women’s sports with the passage of the Fairness in Women’s Sports Act,” Gov. Brad Little said in a written statement. “We are leading the nation in supporting generations of women and men who fought hard to uphold Title IX protections and keep girls and women safe.” ”Today’s ruling is a resounding victory for fairness in women’s sports and for every young woman in Idaho who shows up to the gym, the track, and the pool ready to compete on a level playing field,” Lt. Gov. Scott Bedke said in a written statement. “The Fairness in Women’s Sports Act is about protecting the integrity of women’s athletics and ensuring that decades of progress under Title IX aren’t quietly undone.”

entry.title: Idaho unveils new state park with ‘endless outdoor recreation opportunities’ in Salmon area
entry.summary: Originally published June 17 on IdahoEdNews.org. There’s good news for those who wish to imbibe during the Fo… The Idaho Department of Parks and Recreation began the process for acquiring and setting up Twin Peaks Ranch State Park near Salmon at the end of 2024. Originally published June 25 on IdahoCapitalSun.com. Idaho welcomed a new state park, the Twin Peaks Ranch State Park, to its statewide network on Saturday to connect recreation and conservation areas in central Idaho. The park, located 20 miles outside Salmon, covers 677 acres of camping grounds, trails, rental units and access to public lands for visitors. Its proximity to the Salmon River and Salmon-Challis National Forest make it what a June 18 press release called a “gateway to endless outdoor recreation opportunities.” “It’s going to be a destination for people with all sorts of outdoor recreation backgrounds, from off-highway vehicle enthusiasts to hikers, disc golfers, fishermen, bicyclists, so on and so forth,” Idaho Department of Parks and Recreation Communications Manager William Armbruster said in an interview with the Idaho Capital Sun. “It’s really a one-stop shop and a gateway to an incredible opportunity for recreation in the Salmon-Challis area.” Idaho Parks and Recreation hosted an opening ceremony for the park on Saturday, giving visitors a chance to meet park staff and learn about the region. The event also included food trucks, local vendors and musician Muzzie Braun from the Braun Brothers Reunion. The ribbon-cutting ceremony took place from 10 a.m. to 5 p.m. For more information on the Twin Peaks Ranch State Park, or its opening celebration, visit parksandrecreation.idaho.gov. Idaho law requires the Department of Parks and Recreation to find suitable areas for state parks that can foster natural preservation and recreation activities, according to Idaho Parks and Recreation Director Susan Buxton. The process, usually including discussions with community members and an assessment of land offers, can take multiple years to complete. The Twin Peaks Ranch park started as a functional — then guest — ranch in the 1990s, but Buxton said community input led Parks and Recreation to consider establishing a formal state park in the area. After talks with legislators, the county commissioners and previous owners, Parks and Recreation began the process for acquiring and setting up the park at the end of 2024. “This property had already been built out, it had a bridge over the Salmon River that had been recently updated,” Buxton said. “It seemed like it was a good opportunity to take a look at something that would not be a huge build out for the state.” Park acquisition funding comes from department’s capital development budget The park acquisition, filed around October 2025, cost the agency $4.75 million including property and assets in the area, according to Armbruster. The funds for acquisitions come from the department’s capital development budget, a dedicated sum distributed by the Idaho Legislature every fiscal year. In 2025, the department counted on close to $37 million for capital outlay destined to acquisitions. This fiscal year, which ends June 30, the capital development budget dropped to $4.4 million, according to House Bill 419. Now, and through the passing of Senate Bill 1417 in March, the capital development budget once again was up to $12.8 million for the 2027 fiscal year. Buxton also said the acquisition included a payment in-lieu of taxes ordinance agreed upon with Lemhi County. Payments in-lieu of taxes compensate local governments for lost tax revenues on the property. The lump sum in-lieu payment totaled $105,838.50, based on a previously assessed value, according to the minutes for a Department of Parks and Recreation quarterly board meeting held Nov. 4, 2025. Throughout the park establishment process, Buxton said Parks and Recreation kept a close eye at the needs of the community. “You have to do a lot of due diligence looking at the needs of the state,” she said. “You do a lot of looking and analyzing: ‘Where would we need (a park)? Could it be someplace that would have the most opportunity or ability to be a good addition to the park system?’” Buxton said the Twin Peaks Ranch fit that criteria, as it included facilities well-suited to recreation, such as lodges, equestrian zones and trails.

entry.title: Idaho state liquor stores to remain open on Fourth of July
entry.summary: Originally published June 25 on IdahoCapitalSun.com. Canyon County declared a local disaster emergency after a se… Bottles of alcoholic drinks line the shelves at an Idaho State Liquor Store in Nampa on Wednesday, Feb. 2, 2022. Liquor stores across Idaho will be open on the Fourth of July and closed on July 5. There’s good news for those who wish to imbibe during the Fourth of July: Idaho state liquor stores will remain open on the federal holiday. All stores operated by the Idaho State Liquor Division will be open on Saturday, July 4, the division announced. Contract stores might be open; visit the store locator on idaholiquor.com to see a full list of stores and hours. State liquor stores will be closed, however, on Sunday, July 5, in observance of America’s 250th birthday. Contract stores might be open. According to the Idaho Liquor Division, the remaining holiday schedule for 2026 is as follows: Independence Day (Saturday, July 4): All state-run stores are open during regular hours. Contract stores might be open. Labor Day (Monday, Sept. 7): All state-run stores are open during regular hours. Contract stores might be open. Columbus Day/Indigenous People’s Day (Monday, Oct. 12): All state-run stores are open during regular hours. Contract stores might be open. Veterans Day (Wednesday, Nov. 11): All state-run stores are open during regular hours. Contract stores might be open. Thanksgiving Day (Thursday, Nov. 26): All state-run stores are closed, and there are no sales at contract stores. Christmas Eve (Thursday, Dec. 24): All state-run stores open at 10 a.m. and close at 6 p.m. Contract stores might be open. Christmas Day (Friday, Dec. 25): All state-run stores are closed, and there are no sales at contract stores. New Year’s Eve (Thursday, Dec. 31): All state-turn stores are open during regular hours. Contract stores might be open. New Year’s Day (Friday, Jan. 1, 2027): All state-run stores are closed. Contract stores might be open. Madison Guernsey is the News Editor at the Idaho Press. entry.title: University of Idaho to offer 3 degrees in AI studies
entry.summary: Meridian native Tyus Sparks was taken in the fourth round of… BOISE — St. Luke’s crossed a significant benchmark Friday mo… Graduate researchers Sarah Davis (left) and Hunter Hawkins (center) work with research faculty John Shovic to find AI solutions for a safety and productivity issue at Amalgamated Sugar. MOSCOW — The University of Idaho is introducing three degrees in artificial intelligence starting this fall. The UI announced Thursday that students will be able to pursue a bachelor’s degree, master of science or master of engineering in AI at the Moscow and Coeur d’Alene campuses. Exploring AI has been a priority for the university, which plans to incorporate the technology “across every major area of the institution,” UI said in a news release. The UI says it is the only institution in Idaho conducting AI research through the National Artificial Intelligence Research Resource (NAIRR) Pilot program, which is operated through the National Science Foundation to train educators in AI. The news release cited a statistic from the U.S. Department of Education that says AI employment opportunities are expected to increase by 17.3% nationwide by 2034. The university currently offers 14 AI courses covering machine learning, deep learning, natural language processing, data science, adversarial machine learning, and computer vision and optimization. entry.title: Law enforcement to increase impaired-driving patrols Fourth of July week
entry.summary: CALDWELL — Construction is officially in progress that will … The USDA issued a public health alert for Private Selection … The Idaho Transportation Department’s Office of Highway Safety is partnering with law enforcement agencies across the state to increase impaired-driving patrols from Sunday, June 28, through Sunday, July 5. Independence Day — and America’s 250th birthday — are occasions worth of celebrating. Driving impaired isn’t worth anything. That’s the message from the Idaho Transportation Department’s Office of Highway Safety, which is partnering with law enforcement agencies across the state to ramp up efforts to stop impaired drivers. The high-visibility patrols will begin Sunday, June 28, and last through Sunday, July 5, ITD said. The Office of Highway Safety urges Idahoans to plan ahead by choosing a designated driver. The OHS cites a number of impaired-driving statistics including: “Drivers aged 30 and under are involved in impaired driving crashes at a disproportionately high rate,” Josephine Middleton, Highway Safety Manager with OHS, said in ITD’s news release. “It’s encouraging to see fewer fatalities in some areas, but the numbers show the problem is far from solved.” “As we look ahead to America’s 250th birthday, it’s worth remembering that nothing is more patriotic than protecting the people in your own community,” Middleton continued. “Choosing not to drive impaired is one of the simplest ways to keep your neighbors safe.” Madison Guernsey is the News Editor at the Idaho Press. entry.title: USDA issues health alert for chicken sold at Idaho Fred Meyer stores
entry.summary: An ACLU lawsuit over the detainment of 400 attendees at a Wi… Payette County authorities are investigating the suspected d… The USDA’s Food Safety and Inspection Service has issued a public health alert for mislabeled raw chicken-breast products containing egg, a known allergen, as an undeclared ingredient on the label, seen here. The USDA’s Food Safety and Inspection Service has issued a public health alert for mislabeled raw chicken-breast products containing egg, a known allergen, as an undeclared ingredient on the label. The product was shipped to Fred Meyer grocery stores in Idaho, along with Fred Meyer and Kroger shops in Alabama, Alaska, Illinois, Indiana, Kentucky, Tennessee, Oregon and Washington, FSIS said in a news release issued late Thursday afternoon. Of concern is 21-ounce vacuum-packed packages of Private Selection Honey Dijon Boneless Skinless Chicken Breasts with Rib Meat. The packages have a best-if-used-by date of June 28, 2026, a lot code of 15326A and an establishment number of P-45288B. The chicken was produced June 2 by FW Farms of Fort Worth, Texas. “The problem was discovered by a retailer after a store employee identified that the correct label was applied to the front of the product but the incorrect label, which contained the ingredients statement, was applied to the back of the product,” FSIS said. A recall was not requested because the product is no longer available; however, it might remain in purchasers’ refrigerators or freezers. If you have this product, FSIS says to throw it away or return it to the place of purchase. Consumers with questions regarding the public health alert can contact FW Farms customer service at . Eggs are one of the most common food allergies among children, according to the Mayo Clinic. Symptoms include skin rashes, hives, a stuffy nose, upset stomach and vomiting, all of which can be mild to serious and can start within minutes to a few hours after consumption. Anaphylaxis is also possible, which can be fatal. Most children outgrow egg allergies by the age of 16, the Mayo Clinic said. What follows is a list of all Fred Meyer grocery stores in Idaho, according to the company. • Glenwood/Chinden: 5425 W. Chinden Blvd. • Fairview Avenue: 1850 E. Fairview Ave. • Yellowstone Avenue: 800 Yellowstone Ave. • Blue Lakes Boulevard: 705 Blue Lakes Blvd. N. Trevor Hawes is the executive editor of the Idaho Press. entry.title: New report finds tax cuts benefit highest-income Idaho households most
entry.summary: New Idaho laws taking effect July 1 will change rules involv… BOISE — The U.S. Supreme Court unanimously ruled in favor of… The Capitol dome stands against the Boise foothills, Tuesday, April 7, 2026. Originally published June 18 on IdahoCapitalSun.com. A new report published this month by a Boise-based nonprofit organization found that state and federal income tax cuts have benefited the highest-income Idaho households the most, while low-income families are actually paying more. The Idaho Center for Fiscal Policy published the findings in the new report, “How federal and state tax cuts shift costs onto Idaho families and communities.” The report’s author, Policy Analyst May Roberts, tracked $4.5 billion in annual state and federal income tax cuts that Idaho households have received since 2018. The report found that 73% of the benefits of those cuts went to the top 20% of households, those that earn $143,000 or more each year. The report also found that Idahoans with the lowest incomes – those earning less than $29,200 annually – saw their taxes increase by an average of $96 per year. Since 2021, the Idaho Legislature has enacted several tax cuts that have reduced state revenue. Here are a few examples: The increase in taxes among the lowest-income earners was due, in part, to the Idaho Legislature allowing the state child tax credit to expire and the federal One Big Beautiful Bill Act not extending premium tax credits that helped Idaho families and small business owners afford health insurance, the report stated. “Tax cuts can be a valuable tool for helping families make ends meet, but it is important to look at who benefits and who bears the costs,” Roberts said in a written statement. “A fair tax system can provide meaningful tax relief while maintaining the essential services Idaho families rely on. When tax cuts significantly reduce the resources available for public services, the costs do not disappear — they are shifted onto families and local communities through higher property taxes, rising tuition and fees, reduced services and growing barriers to accessing healthcare and food assistance.” Sen. Scott Grow, a Republican from Eagle who serves as co-chair of the Idaho Legislature’s Joint Finance-Appropriations Committee, supports the tax cuts. “I am a fiscal conservative,” Grow said. “As (certified public accountants), we try to help our clients to make sure they pay a fair amount of tax and not overly pay. What we are hearing from our constituents is that they are concerned about high taxes, whether it be property tax or income tax. We try to be very fiscally conservative. I believe in limited government. We need to fund the government appropriately, and I feel like we do that.” The report also found that the combined $4.5 billion in tax cuts exceeds the Idaho Legislature’s fiscal year 2026 state funding for K-12 public education, colleges and universities and health and human services. The report said that for fiscal year 2026, there was $2.2 billion in state funding for K-12 public education, $1.2 billion for health and human services and $492 million for colleges and universities. After the Idaho Legislature cut funding for colleges in universities in fiscal year 2026 and fiscal year 2027, the Idaho State Board of Education approved tuition increases, Idaho Education News reported. “These examples illustrate how state-level budget cuts can shift costs to local governments and directly onto Idahoans,” the Idaho Center for Fiscal Policy’s new report states. More information about the Idaho Center for Fiscal Policy and its research is available online. Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. Sign up now! To view our latest e-Edition, click the image on the left.

entry.title: Olympian skier Bode Miller pleads not guilty to Idaho misdemeanor drug charges
entry.summary: Originally published June 18 on IdahoCapitalSun.com. The Idaho GOP concluded last week’s convention with a party … USA men’s ski team member and six-time Olympic medalist Bode Miller participates in a news conference at the alpine skiing world championships Feb. 2, 2015, in Beaver Creek, Colo. United States’ Bode Miller makes a jump during men’s downhill combined training at the Sochi 2014 Winter Olympics, Feb. 13, 2014, in Krasnaya Polyana, Russia. Olympic gold medalist skier Bode Miller has pleaded not guilty to a pair of misdemeanor drug charges after he was arrested on a charge of possessing psilocybin mushrooms. Miller was arrested June 6 in eastern Idaho, according to court records, and pleaded not guilty to possession of a controlled substance and possession of drug paraphernalia the following week. Miller said in a post on Instagram that he was pulled over after accelerating to pass a vehicle on the highway. His friend had a small amount of cannabis and a cannabis pipe, which Miller said he didn’t know about. “We fully cooperated with the officer,” he said. “I am hopeful the misdemeanor charges will be dropped once the facts are reviewed.” The court documents don’t include any details about the circumstances surrounding Miller’s arrest. But in a probable cause statement, Fremont County Sheriff’s Deputy Jacob Hurt wrote that he found Miller with a white dispensary bag containing 4.1 grams of the psychedelic mushrooms. Idaho has some of the strictest drug laws in the nation. But Colorado and Oregon have both legalized the use of psilocybin for therapeutic treatments, and the substance has become increasingly popular with some health advocates who believe that microdosing it or using it in therapeutic settings can help ease anxiety, depression and post-traumatic stress disorder. In April, President Donald Trump signed an executive order directing the Food and Drug Administration and other federal agencies to speed up research and loosen restrictions on psychedelics, and in response the FDA said it would offer ultra-fast review to three psychedelic drugs being developed to treat mental health conditions. The 48-year-old Miller took a gambler’s approach to ski racing. His high-risk, high-reward style resulted in six Olympic medals, including gold in the super-combined at the 2010 Vancouver Games, and numerous crashes. His last major race was at the 2015 world championships in Beaver Creek, Colorado, when a bad wipeout knocked him out of the super-G. He cut a gate too close and hooked his left arm, sending him spiraling out of control. His skis popped off, and he began to somersault down the slope before righting himself. He slowly got up and waited for someone to bring him his skis. Once he clicked back into the skis, he went down the slope and waved to the fans. Miller later underwent surgery to fix a torn right hamstring tendon caused when his ski sliced him. Miller won 33 World Cup races and a pair of World Cup overall titles. He also captured four gold medals at world championships. Copyright 2026 The Associated Press. All rights reserved. entry.title: Idaho GOP platform seeks to end property taxes, cut ties with 'progressive' school accreditors
entry.summary: New Idaho laws taking effect July 1 will change rules involv… BOISE — The U.S. Supreme Court unanimously ruled in favor of… Members of the Idaho GOP’s resolution committee convened Friday, June 19, to advance, or reject, a range of proposals that either made direct calls for future legislation or merely reaffirmed the party’s stance on certain key issues. The Idaho GOP concluded last week’s convention with a party platform that mostly resembles the planks adopted in 2024. Two relatively small additions amongst a more-than 30-page document, however, could change the tax structure in the state as well as the standards that need to be met by Idaho schools. An end to property taxesIn what represents a substantial change in approach to taxation, the updated Idaho GOP platform is now calling for the elimination of property taxes in their entirety. Though an elimination of both property taxes and income taxes were recommended out of the Republican platform committee last week, language calling for the elimination of the income tax was not included on the party’s final platform. Providing specific instruction for Republican members of the Idaho Legislature, the platform calls for “a plan to fully replace property tax revenue with alternative funding sources that do not threaten homeownership.” Expanding on this further in a plank on property rights, the platform states property tax is “incompatible with true private property ownership.” This differs from the party’s platform adopted in 2024, which stated a belief that a “combination of our income, sales, and property taxes will continue to provide a stable, dependable source of income for governmental needs,” while also encouraging a “reduction in property taxes whenever possible.” If such a wholesale elimination of property taxes were pursued by the legislature, it would significantly alter the revenue landscape for municipalities across Idaho. Since the state doesn’t receive any property tax, the change in revenue stream would fall to Idaho cities, counties and local taxing districts which each rely on property taxes to fund infrastructure and essential services. According to 2024 data compiled by the Tax Foundation, a nonpartisan tax policy think tank, Idaho’s effective property tax rate continues to rank among the lowest in the nation at 0.5%. The Idaho State Tax Commission similarly found Idaho’s property tax burden ranks 45 out of 51 when including Washington, D.C., and adjusting for differences in income and population among states. Though Idaho’s property tax rate is already among the lowest in the nation, it sits only behind sales taxes as the second-largest category of taxes collected in the state. A 2024 Annual Report from the Idaho State Tax Commission, the most recent year such a report is available, reported property taxes in Idaho accounted for $2.2 billion, or 25% of all state and local taxes collected that year. From this amount, cities and counties each received more than $600 million in revenue. A further $439 million was distributed to schools districts, $145 million was distributed to road and highway districts and $134 million was distributed to fire districts, along with the the remainder going to over a dozen other local services and institutions. Kelley Packer, executive director of the Association of Idaho Cities (AIC), the nonpartisan lobbying group advocating on behalf of Idaho’s 197 incorporated cities, said eliminating property taxes would result in cities experiencing a “major breakdown of infrastructure.” Since the passage of House Bill 389 in 2021, which capped the rate at which local governments could increase their annual budgets, Packer highlighted the law has broadly resulted in increased competition between cities, counties and taxing districts for a limited amount of revenue to fund their respective needs. At the extreme end, the lack of revenues can result in cities disincorporating. Packer said three cities attempted to disincorporate last year, but federal grants were able to help bridge the gap to keep them whole. She noted when cities opt to disincorporate, as Bingham County’s Atomic City did in 2020, the costs for streets, first responders and other services don’t go away — they are simply pushed onto the counties that are themselves contending with limited tax revenues. Even though property taxes are presented as competing with the ability to own one’s home, Packer underscored Idahoans do own their homes — just not the infrastructure and associated upkeep to service a home. Packer also noted property taxes are of particular importance during times of economic downturn, as they present a level of stability during unfavorable economic conditions while income and sales taxes tend to be more variable. This typically allows for municipalities to continue to deliver on their existing services, whether it be firefighters or wastewater or roads, despite other revenues declining. “We’ve either got to recognize that there’s a cost there, and be willing to share that burden as a whole,” Packer said, “or those services have to go away.” A potential change in school accreditation Beyond a tax overhaul, the refreshed party platform also includes new direction for the Idaho Legislature on the state’s future relationship with certain accrediting bodies — which act independently to evaluate educational rigor — at both the K-12 and post-secondary levels. Through an amendment of Idaho code, the platform directs the legislature to sever ties with any educational accreditor that “promotes ideological agendas antithetical to the values and principles of the Idaho Republican Party Platform” for several issues including “social justice indoctrination, diversity, equity and inclusion frameworks, or progressive gender ideology.”

entry.title: Developer partnering with Coeur d'Alene Tribe on project aiming to shift Treasure Valley's border eastward
entry.summary: Thousands attended the 60th God and Country Festival in Namp… At 19, Joshua Ritter, born in Moscow to parents Robert and S… The Peregrine will be arriving in Elmore County near two planned residential communities and a future Sho-Pai Casino and Resort. BOISE — Meridian-based developer Ahlquist and the Coeur d’Alene Tribe have announced the Peregrine, a future, mixed-use development that will push the border of the Treasure Valley eastward as residential development in the area continues forward. The 920-acre mixed-use project will be located in Elmore County and in close proximity to both the Boise Airport and Micron’s Boise campus. The development will offer industrial, commercial, service, retail and residential uses and is set to feature a 774-acre industrial and technology park as well as a 146-acre commercial park. The park is currently available for sale or lease and is intending to create a “new eastern border of the Treasure Valley,” according to a press release from the Coeur d’Alene Tribe and Ahlquist, who have formed a partnership to bring the development forward. “Ahlquist is honored to partner with the Coeur d’Alene Tribe on a project of this magnitude,” Ahlquist CEO Tommy Ahlquist said in the release. “As the eastern edge of the Treasure Valley continues to grow, we believe Peregrine will play an important role in shaping the future of this corridor.” The site located off Interstate 84 at the Simco Road interchange will be across from two planned residential communities in Mayfield, Idaho, that are expected to bring more than 10,000 homes to the area. The development will also sit close to the future Sho-Pai Resort and Casino, which is similarly being developed in partnership with the Coeur d’Alene Tribe, the release said. A rendering of the 920-acre Peregrine development. The mixed-use project is the product of a partnership with Ahlquist and the Coeur d''Alene Tribe and will feature a commercial park as well as an industrial and technology park. “A project of this size and ambition reflects the Coeur d’Alene Tribe’s long-standing commitment to thoughtful, lasting economic development, and we are excited to help bring it to the Treasure Valley,” Chief Allan, chairman of the Coeur d’Alene Tribe, said. “This development is an opportunity to meet the needs of Idaho business owners, while also bringing new vitality to the area where we are in the process of helping the Shoshone-Paiute Tribes build their first and only resort and casino.” The development announcement arrives as Micron’s Boise campus, located 12 minutes from the mixed-use site, has expanded significantly in recent years alongside the artificial intelligence boom. Last year, in a joint announcement with the Trump administration, Micron announced a $200 billion expansion across the U.S., including $30 billion for Boise’s second memory fabrication plant. “Micron’s investment in Idaho will drive transformational economic growth and create tremendous opportunities across the region,” Ahlquist said in the release. We’re proud to provide a location that can support and accommodate that expansion.” The Boise Airport, approximately 15 minutes away from the Peregrine, has similarly grown alongside the Treasure Valley and is continuing to work toward a more than $40 million concourse expansion featuring up to 10 additional gates to account for the record-breaking number of enplanements observed year after year. Ahlquist has been the developer of several large-scale, mixed-use developments in recent years. In May of last year, Boise’s 4th and Idaho development held a “topping-off” ceremony to mark the progress on a two-tower, 13-story development offering for-sale condominiums. Last month, Ahlquist began the construction phase of Meridian’s The District at Ten Mile, a 200-acre development that will feature In-N-Out, Panera and Target, among others. Royce McCandless is the Statehouse reporter and covers Idaho politics. You can email him at . Sign up today for one of our great newsletters and get headlines right in your inbox every morning. entry.title: Boise Centre considers River Street expansion
entry.summary: Gov. Brad Little proclaimed July 2 as Wildland Firefighter D… BOISE — A persistent dry spell has inspired an ordinance out… The entrance to the Boise Centre is pictured in this file photo. BOISE — With land purchased and intentions to expand made clear, the Boise Centre is now facing a question — what do we build? Boise’s premiere event venue has been considering the area’s growth in its plans for an expansion and has purchased two parcels this year slated for a new facility. The first parcel was purchased in early April and is a 4-acre lot. It sits near the western corner of the 10th and River Street intersection. The second parcel, a 1.8-acre lot, was acquired at the end of April and is just across the street, east of 10th Street, according to a press release. A commercial building stands at the northeast corner of 11th and River streets in Boise, Tuesday, April 7, 2026. The Greater Boise Auditorium District purchased the 4-acre property — plus an additional 1.8-acre parcel — to serve as a stepping stone for the expansion of the Boise Centre. “We’re focused on building a solid foundation for both the district and greater downtown Boise that will withstand needs well into the future,” Greater Boise Auditorium District Board Chair Kristin Muchow said in the release. “This allows us to move forward and evaluate the best use of the land to serve our community and deliver economic impact to benefit the city.” At a Tuesday, May 26 meeting in the Boise Centre, the Greater Boise Auditorium District Board, which operates the center, heard from Johnson Consulting on options for growth. Project Manager John Fleming recapped discussions that have occurred over the past several months. A 2025 study by Johnson Consulting found that the center loses an average of 115 events annually due to a lack of available space. At a current size of 86,000 square feet in event space, the Boise Centre is limited by the number of events it is capable of hosting at once. Recommendations by Johnson Consulting include suggestions to double its current size. A consideration on the table is typically to expand an existing facility. For the Boise Centre in the middle of downtown, Fleming said the expansion is a challenge, with few available parcels nearby. The center could be expanded vertically, but the expansion would be limited. This naturally led to plans for a new facility on a secondary campus. Proposals have been narrowed down to three main options. The first option would consider constructing a ground-level, 60,000-square-foot exhibit hall as well as a second-story, 40,000-square-foot exhibit hall. A second option is to only construct a 50,000-square-foot exhibit hall. Both options would support a moderate attendance increase. Fleming noted that conventions and conferences at the Boise Centre are the event types that bring the greatest number of hotel stays. At the same time, the center could do better. “It’s not to say that by adding 50,000 square feet of exhibit space or 100,000 square feet broken across two levels you’re not achieving any growth,” Fleming said. “You’re achieving very substantial growth. It’s just, less than maybe what we see as the market opportunity.” The third option presented is a two-phase plan that would begin with constructing a 100,000-square-foot exhibit hall. In phase two, the center would also add 40,000 square feet in ballrooms and 20,000 square feet in meeting rooms. Also in discussion was the profitability of sports tourism. The board has considered building a multi-sport complex that can host multiple games at a time. “We see very frequently large exhibit halls hosting major volumes of sports tourism,” Fleming said. “We would see that as being certainly a part — if a satellite facility were to be built that was centered around an exhibit hall — certainly a part of that equation.”

entry.title: Micron milestone: Memory chip maker reaches $1 trillion market cap
entry.summary: Gov. Brad Little proclaimed July 2 as Wildland Firefighter D… BOISE — A persistent dry spell has inspired an ordinance out… Micron Technology in Boise, Monday, June 10, 2024. The semiconductor company’s stock price closed the trading day Tuesday up 19.29% to $895.88 per share, giving the semiconductor company a $1 trillion market cap. BOISE — Welcome to the trillion dollar club, Micron. Boise-headquartered Micron Technology’s stock price closed the trading day Tuesday up 19.29% to $895.88 per share, giving the semiconductor company a $1 trillion market cap. It marks a new high valuation for Micron and puts it in the ranks of the handful of trillion-dollar companies that includes Nvidia, Microsoft, Apple, Alphabet, Meta, Broadcom, Tesla, TSMC and Berkshire Hathaway. Micron closed Friday at $751 per share. Its stock price jumped in pre-market trading to open Tuesday $820.50, buoyed by an announcement from UBS that the investment bank had raised its target price for Micron to $1,625 per share — a tripling of its previous target of $535 set in April. “We believe the market will start to put a more ‘normal’ multiple on the stock and MU will continue to re-rate higher as more details emerge about the structural changes AI has driven to the entire memory complex,” UBS wrote in its analysis. The AI data center race has generated strong demand for the memory chips and storage products Micron and only a handful of others make. The demand is so strong that the company announced in December 2025 its exit from the consumer memory business. “The AI-driven growth in the data center has led to a surge in demand for memory and storage. Micron has made the difficult decision to exit the Crucial (brand) consumer business in order to improve supply and support for our larger, strategic customers in faster-growing segments,” the company said in a statement at the time. Micron is the only memory manufacturer based in the United States. Micron began in 1978 as a four-person semiconductor design company in the basement of a Boise dental office, according to a company timeline. Two years later, it broke ground on its first fabrication site in Boise. In June 2025, the company announced a $200 billion investment to expand domestic memory manufacturing and research and development, estimating the creation of 90,000 jobs. Micron’s plans include investing in two leading-edge, high-volume fabrication facilities in Idaho, which will create more than 17,000 new jobs, the company said. entry.title: Terrace Drive-In will open this summer after all
entry.summary: Thousands attended the 60th God and Country Festival in Namp… At 19, Joshua Ritter, born in Moscow to parents Robert and S… A sign directs patrons to the concession area at the Terrace Drive-In theater, Thursday, May 21, 2026. The main screen and viewing area at Terrace Drive-In theater, Thursday, May 21, 2026. The main screen and viewing area at the Terrace Drive-In theater, Thursday, May 21, 2026. CALDWELL — The nostalgic longtime Terrace Drive-In movie theater will indeed have outdoor showings for the 2026 season and is set to open in July under new management. The concession/projection building and main screen at the Terrace Drive-In theater, Thursday, May 21, 2026. Being one of only two drive-in movie theaters in the Treasure Valley, Terrace Drive-In has become a staple for community members who look forward to the opening season each year. The drive-in, located at 4011 S. Lake Ave. in Caldwell, has been family-owned and operated for 35 years. But its history dates back to the 1950s when it was first built. It now remains one of the last few drive-in theaters in Idaho. Alice Estrada purchased the drive-in theater in 1991 and fully operated it herself until 2018. Since then, it has gone through multiple changes in operations management within the family, with a new operator ready to take charge as of late spring. Weeds grow through the pavement at the former entrance to the Terrace Drive-In theater, Thursday, May 21, 2026. The changes caused miscommunication between the former and incoming operators in March, leading to an inaccurate statement on the drive-in’s social media accounts informing the community that the drive-in was not going to be opening for the 2026 season due to incomplete renovations. Those social media posts reached hundreds of heartbroken community members who did not want to see another local drive-in’s existence disappear. Many of them offered ways to extend their support in the renovation efforts so that the drive-in could reopen. Numbered rows in the main viewing area at Terrace Drive-In theater, Thursday, May 21, 2026. As the property owner, Estrada told the Idaho Press that she wanted to apologize to the community and families who look forward to the drive-in’s opening and said the drive-in would be moving forward no matter what. “I apologize, not for something I did, but I still need to apologize because of the (misinformation) that was distributed through the internet,” Estrada said. While the drive-in remains closed and will have a shorter season than normal by opening from the middle of summer until late October, attendees can expect an uplifting experience with the new additions and renovations currently underway. The later-than-usual opening date is due to permit requirements, renovations and transition between operators, according to Dera Poynter, the drive-in’s new operator and family friend of Estrada, who also said she has been reviewing guests’ concerns and is exploring ways to improve their outdoor movie theater experience. The bright color of the concession and projection building stands out as the centerpiece of the Terrace Drive-In theater, Thursday, May 21, 2026. “The goal is to bring back the classic Terrace Drive-In experience people remember while refreshing concessions and adding some new energy,” Poynter said.

entry.title: Mini-subdivision approved in Traverse Creek Subdivision in Caldwell
entry.summary: Thousands attended the 60th God and Country Festival in Namp… At 19, Joshua Ritter, born in Moscow to parents Robert and S… Caldwell City Hall in downtown Caldwell. CALDWELL — Just over a one-acre parcel will be added to an already approved subdivision in Caldwell. At Monday’s city council meeting, the council unanimously voted to approve the annexation of a 1.07-acre parcel and a preliminary plat for the Traverse Landing Subdivision — which will add six homes to the 265 that are planned for the larger Traverse Creek Subdivision. The approved project was the first and smallest application request the council approved of the night. The approved subdivision will be located near North Ranch Business Park — an area of the city that has seen large amounts of development over recent years. Engineering consulting firm NV5, on behalf of Endurance Holdings LLC, received a green light to add an additional parcel to their existing home project. The approved parcel is located on the north side of Marble Front Road near the KCID Road intersection and will be zoned as R-2, or community residential. Traverse Landing Subdivision will be a single-family residential subdivision with six buildable lots and one common lot. It was unanimously approved by the city’s planning and zoning commission on April 8. It will fit within the applicant’s already approved larger project, Traverse Creek Subdivision, according to project representative Bonnie Layton. CBH Homes will build the Traverse Creek Subdivision after it was approved by the city council in 2024 and is expected to be coming soon, according to the homebuilder’s website, as it is being completed in phases. Traverse Creek Subdivision was approved to bring in 265 detached single-family homes and 14 townhomes, according to the city’s senior planner, Katie Wright. The six-lot subdivision will be built in phase one of the larger project’s six-phase plan. The Vallivue School District raised concerns about the subdivision, adding pressure to Skyway Elementary School on Ward Road, though it is not at capacity yet. Council Member Chris Allgood questioned Layton as to how one parcel was left behind when the Traverse Creek Subdivision was approved two years ago. “That happens at times — a property owner isn’t willing to sell at a certain point and for whatever reason their timing changes,” Layton said. “I’ve seen it happen time and again where somebody decided they are finally ready to move to someplace else or their life circumstances change.” Council Member Mike Dittenber made a motion to approve all three action items, followed by a second from council members Geoff Williams and Allgood. Mariela Esquivel-Rodriguez is a reporter for the Idaho Press focused on Caldwell and Canyon County.
